teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the excitement of seeing the real robot move, then immediately connect that excitement to disciplined observation.
teaching_arc.progression: Move through hardware realities, demonstrations, and the differences between simulated and real behavior.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish with a better sense of what changes when the robot leaves simulation and enters the real lab.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Connect the simulation-first course material to the behavior and constraints students see on the real TurtleBot3 platform.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand what transfers cleanly from simulation to the real robot and what does not.
Students should see the importance of calibration, sensing limits, and operating discipline on hardware.
Students should leave with a stronger mental bridge between software commands and physical robot behavior.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep energy slightly higher on real-robot demonstrations.
Always connect the visible behavior back to a software or sensing reason.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slides.narration_seed: Today is not deep theory. It’s a practical demo so you know what the system looks like in real life.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Connect the simulation-first course material to the behavior and constraints students see on the real TurtleBot3 platform.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into How today works (important) so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Frame TurtleBot3 (Real Robot) — End-to-End Demo as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how TurtleBot3 (Real Robot) — End-to-End Demo sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot model, TF, and RViz displays as a simple pipeline so students can connect each layer to the visual outcome.

slides.narration_seed: Use How today works (important) to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.

must_cover: Confirm you are on the same Wi-Fi as the instructor laptop

Disable firewall temporarily (common DDS issue):
If still nothing: stop here and just follow the demo visually

slides.narration_seed: Teach Mapping tips (make the map usable) through the visual or interactive material, narrating what students should notice, why it matters, and how it connects to the topic goal.
must_cover: Move slowly (avoid sudden spins and fast acceleration)
Scan corners and doorway edges (don’t miss boundaries)
Try to “loop back” to where you started (helps reduce drift)
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
If map looks stretched: pause, re-scan area, and loop-close again

slide_ref.source_title: Occupancy Grid Map (OGM) — what the map means

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for Occupancy Grid Map (OGM) — what the map means using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: Saved maps are 2D occupancy grids
White = free space
Black = occupied (walls/objects)
Gray = unknown (not scanned yet)
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into Step 7 (optional): Start navigation using the saved map so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for Occupancy Grid Map (OGM) — what the map means first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture Occupancy Grid Map (OGM) — what the map means clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: Define RViz navigation tools (what you click) in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Set initial pose: click 2D Pose Estimate, then click/drag on the map
Send a goal: click 2D Nav Goal, then click/drag destination
The arrow sets the goal orientation (θ)
Source: TB3 Navigation → RViz goal

slide_ref.source_title: Project hint (no deep coding): how to do goals programmatically

slides.narration_seed: Define Project hint (no deep coding): how to do goals programmatically in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Nav2 provides an action interface (NavigateToPose / FollowWaypoints)
Your node will publish/send a goal pose (x, y, yaw) in the map frame
Start with RViz to learn coordinates, then move to code
Next sessions: we’ll show the minimal “send a goal” node pattern

slides.narration_seed: Use Quiz: What does map_saver_cli create? as a final self-check so students can tell whether they now understand the core ideas of the topic.
must_cover: When you run ros2 run nav2_map_server map_saver_cli -f ~/tb3_map, you get…
Map saving produces an occupancy image (.pgm) plus config/metadata (.yaml).

slides.narration_seed: Use Today’s remote-PC command checklist to preview the route of the lesson, explain how this section fits the overall teaching arc, and tell students what to listen for next.
must_cover: Set env: export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=30 + export TURTLEBOT3_MODEL=waffle_pi
Verify topics: ros2 topic list and ros2 topic echo /scan -n 1
RViz: ros2 launch turtlebot3_bringup rviz2.launch.py
Teleop: ros2 run turtlebot3_teleop teleop_keyboard
